speaker: interviewer
text: September 19, 1995. Survivor is Abram Geller. And this is tape three. Tell me about what you did to check what happened to your family.

speaker: interviewee
text: As soon it was possible, I hijacked on a truck. The only transportation was a military truck. I went from Plaszow to [POLISH] and from [POLISH] to Lutsk. I stopped a Lutsk for a reason, because that was my mother's birthplace. And before the war, I used to come to visit my grandfather and grandmother. They used to have their own home. It was a family of my grandfather, grandmother, mother. My mother had two sisters and a brother there. They all lived in that house. And I stopped. I went over to the neighbor. And he told me they all got killed during the ghetto in Lutsk. And he even remembered my mother had, had a grandfather. I guess he was about 93. And he got killed also. That was my first visit in Lustk after the war. And I had already a feeling that no good news awaited me in Wlodzimierz. Then next, I went from Lutsk to Wlodzimierz. And I went to the place where we used to live. My house was still there. People were living there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Gentiles? Gentiles were living in your house?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. I didn't know who was living there. But I just couldn't walk into that house. They were strange people to me. I walked to my neighbor, a Gentile man. He used, he used to buy lumber from us. He used to make, he used to make those-- what do you call it? Caskets. He used to buy the lumber from us. And from him, he knew me very well. He told me the story, that they all perished. Nobody survived.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did he know the conditions, what happened exactly?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. His wife told me that she spoke with my mother. She wanted to take my sister for hiding when things got bad. But my mother didn't want to give it her, didn't agree on that. They all perished. And nobody survived. I was there from the morning until evening. I didn't even want to sleep over there. As soon as I can, I just wanted to leave and get out of there. And I left the same day. I went back to Lutsk and came back to Plaszow. And that was my whole picture. I realized that all I'm left is me and Dina and no family whatsoever.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's when you decided you were going to leave Poland?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well I decided it before that I'm going to leave Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: Before, yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: But in Poland--

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, what did you do?

speaker: interviewee
text: We started to move in that direction. And like I-- after my daughter was born, we left, we left for Germany. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you-- oh, you had difficult time getting there. But you got there.

speaker: interviewee
text: We have difficult time. But you know, there was-- considering the staying of the Germans, that wasn't so difficult. That was a, a road to freedom. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you get help when you got into Germany? Did you get help?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, we went with other, another few couples. Together, we went. But I was only one who had a child. They were, they were my age people. But I had the only one. And it was a little difficult with her. But we made it. And we came to, to Germany. We stayed there in Deggendorf until we got in touch, Dina got in touch with, with her uncles here in, in the States. And they send us affidavit. We came to the United States, I believe, in, in June, 1946.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you remember your arrival?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. The arrival was, was great. We looking, look forward to here. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Remember your feelings when you saw the New York skyline?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you think? What did you feel?

speaker: interviewee
text: Looked on the Statue of Liberty. And, and I realized that I hoped for a, a life, for a normal life in the States. And, and It worked that way. We had a normal life in the States.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you live?

speaker: interviewee
text: We had up and down. But in general, we were happy. We loved the United States. The States were good to us. And we had a good life here.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you think was the attitude of the American Jews to people like yourself?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, you can't say just American Jews. Different people, you know? But they, they just-- the American Jews were concerned with themselves. And they just didn't believe us in the early years when we came over, the things what we told them about, it was true. And they just didn't want to get upset. They didn't want to hear about those stories. So we just kept, kept quiet and got on with our lives.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you tell your children about your experiences?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah. I didn't tell them right away. But when my children started grow up, they realized that we not, were we not born here, that we came from the other side. And we had a hard life and, and became many miracles. And we survived. And we came here. And then when we were on, about 10, 15 years ago, my children insisted I should make a tape of my life. Me and Dina, we both make tapes. And they just realized what, what's all about and what kind of life we have.

speaker: interviewer
text: Have you ever been back to Europe, to Poland?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. We've been, I've been, I've been back, we've been back to Europe. But I would never go back to Poland. I'll never go back to Poland or to Germany. I've been many places in Europe. But to Poland, I don't have a feeling to go back where so much Jewish blood was spilled with their cooperation. Because without their cooperation, a much bigger percentage of Jews would have been alive today. The Germans find in Poland the right place to terminate the Jews, because Polish people collaborated with Germans. It was harder for them to do it in Holland or Denmark [INAUDIBLE] was with the Jews than in Poland. For them, for them was, Poland was the right place. They built all those concentration camps, extermination camps, in Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I would never go back to Poland. I told that to my children. They, my daughter wanted to go back to [POLISH]. That was her birthplace. She was born in Plaszow. But now, Plaszow is Ukraine. You know, and it's no more Poland. So it's up to her if she wants to g, but not me. I am not going there anymore. I know you've got grandchildren. But recently, you got something very special, a great-grandson?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. That's, that's, that's really something what I didn't expect. I didn't expect to live that, that, that I should have a granddaughter, and married, a married granddaughter.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you have-- he's only a little baby. And so he's not ready to hear it yet. But do you have a message for him for the future that you want to say, to record?

speaker: interviewee
text: Just go be straight in your life, like I was all of my life, a straight life. Never hurt anybody. And be honest. That's I conduct my life. And I would do it all over again.

speaker: interviewer
text: [SIGH] I know it's difficult that you told this story. It's not easy. And I want to thank you for doing it.

speaker: interviewee
text: Very welcome. You did a good job.

speaker: interviewer
text: You did.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my wife, Dina. And we are married 51 years. And I hope for some more. And all I can say, she was always good to me. And I hope to live more, many more years together.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have--

speaker: interviewee
text: What else can I say?

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, did you have a--

speaker: interviewee
text: She was always good to me. And that I am sitting here and having the interview, I have-- she has a big part of it. She always gave me the courage. And I would say if not from her, I don't know if I would have want to have the courage to go on and to suffer so much and to be willing to survive. A big part of it is her doing. What else can I say? I love her.

speaker: interviewee
text: He's laughing.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I hope we will keep going on together.

speaker: interviewer
text: You want to say something, Dina?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. It's actually 53 years that we know each other. And we went through hell. But at the end, then God, God paid us for all our, our suffering, that we raised a wonderful family, as I mentioned before. And we hope to live and see some more [NON-ENGLISH]. And to you, I want to thank you for making this tape. You really did a wonderful job. And we both appreciate. You're very nice and very kind.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right. I second the motion.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we really appreciate-- very patient and very nice. And I hope our children will see it someday.

speaker: interviewee
text: They will.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. And let's hope it will never happen again for our future generations. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell us about this picture.

speaker: interviewee
text: In front with the hat is my grandfather, Iankel Geller. And next to it is my aunt, Yetta Geller. Next to Yetta is my father, Solomon Geller, and then my uncle, Gershon Geller. On the lower line to the right is my grandmother and then my sister, Bella, my mother, Renia, and my aunt, Sonia.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was this taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was taken in Wlodzimierz Wolynski in our lumber yard, in the, in back of our lumber yard.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Poland?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Poland, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when?

speaker: interviewee
text: Approximately in, in summer, 1935.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell us about this picture.

speaker: interviewee
text: Second, in the second row from the right.

speaker: interviewer
text: Second row, second from the right.

speaker: interviewee
text: Second from the right is my picture with all my Jewish friends taking an excursion in Lublin.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year?

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell us about this picture.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my father as a boy. I believe it was taken in 1918.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Solomon Geller. And the round picture here is--

speaker: interviewer
text: It's only the--

speaker: interviewee
text: My grandfather.

speaker: interviewer
text: No, no. It's only one picture at a time. Tell us about this picture of you.

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is me and my wife, Dina, taking right after we came to United States in '48, 1948.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where were you?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was in, in Brooklyn, New York.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell us about this picture.

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture is taken in Wieliczka in 1935. It was a excursion, a class excursions, and with my whole class, Jewish class. And I am here in the third row from the bottom, and the second from the left side.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell us about this picture, Abram.

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture was taken on, on 4th of July, 1993, when my granddaughter got married. That's my son, Syd, his wife, Barbara, my granddaughter, [? Lori, ?] and Lucy.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was it taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was taken on the 4th of July.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where?

speaker: interviewee
text: 1993, when my daughter got married, the wedding.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes. But where was the wedding held?

speaker: interviewee
text: The wedding was where, Dina? A, it's in a place in Manhattan. I forgot about the name of it.

speaker: interviewee
text: Long Island.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Tell us about this?

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture was taken on our 50 anniversary in Lawrence, New York. On the left is my, my son-in-law, Michael, my daughter, Doris.

speaker: interviewer
text: What's his surname?

speaker: interviewee
text: My wife, Dina, and me.

speaker: interviewer
text: What's your son-in-law and daughter's surname?

speaker: interviewee
text: Michael. Michael. And my daughter name is Doris.

speaker: interviewer
text: Simon?

speaker: interviewee
text: Simon. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Of course.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when was this taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture sure was taken on our 50 anniversary in Lawrence, New York. From our left is my son, Syd Geller. Next, it's me, Abram Geller. Next is my wife, Dina Geller. Then on the lower row is my granddaughter, Alicia Geller, and [? Lori ?] Geller.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what year was this photo?

speaker: interviewer
text: When was this taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture was taken on our 50 anniversary. That was in August, 1994. To the left, the first one is my granddaughter, Nina Simon, and then my grandson, Mitchell Simon, my wife, Dina Geller, me, Abram Geller. On the bottom is Alicia Geller and my granddaughter, [? Lori ?] Geller. [SPEAKING POLISH]

speaker: interviewee
text: Nina's name.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell us about this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture, that was taken on July 4th, 1994, '93, on the wedding of my granddaughter. That's my granddaughter, Nina [? Carroll. ?] On top is my wife, Dina Geller. On the bottom is my picture, Abram Geller. What's his name, Dina? I forgot.

speaker: interviewee
text: Alex [? Carroll. ?]

speaker: interviewee
text: Alex, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, who is this important person? [LAUGHTER] Who is he?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a picture of my great grandson, Alex [? Carroll, ?] taken in September, 1995.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell us about this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture was taken in Lawrence, New York, on our 50th wedding anniversary. From left is my son, Syd Geller. Next to him is myself, Abram Geller. Next is Dina Geller. Then it's Barbara Geller. On the lower part is my granddaughter, Alicia Geller. And next to her is [? Laurie ?] Geller. That's it.
